content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


July 20, 1998



Ben Williams
Deputy Director, Administration
Governor's Office of Planning and Research
1400 Tenth Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-98-162

Dear Mr. Williams:

     This is in response to your request for advice under the conflict of interest disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Are the members of the Commission on Local Governance for the 21st Century required to
develop a conflict of interest code and file statements of economic interests?

CONCLUSION

No. The Commission on Local Governance for the 21st Century (the "commission")
qualifies for an exemption from the need to develop a conflict of interest code. We are, by way
of this letter, issuing such an exemption to the commission.

DISCUSSION

Every state and local government agency must have a conflict of interest code ("code"). A
conflict of interest code is a set of rules or regulations that sets out the positions (members,
employees) within the agency that make or participate in the making of decisions on behalf of the
agency. The code further sets out specific disclosure requirements of each position named in the
code. The Fair Political Practices Commission is the agency which approves, amends or rejects a
code created by a state agency or any subdivision thereof. (Sections 87300, 87302 and 82011.)

¹Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18100-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. I-98-162
Page 2

Regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission permit it to exempt an agency from
the need to develop a code if certain criteria are met. (Regulation 18751.) The Commission on
Local Governance for the 21st Century meets one such criteria. Regulation 18751(c)(2) exempts
an agency from the need to develop a code if the agency is, or soon will be, inoperative and
nonfunctioning.

The commission was established by Ch. 943, Stats. of 1997, to review current statutes
allowing the formation of local government agencies, review proposals to increase citizen and
community participation in local governments, review local governments' compliance with
various federal laws, solicit the views and advice of the public on such issues and to report its
findings and recommendations to the legislature and governor by June 30, 1999. The first
meeting of the commission occurred in June 1998. Once the commission issues its report and
recommendations on June 30, 1999, it will wind down and sunset on January 1, 2000, unless its
existence is extended by statute.²

In accordance with Regulation 18751(c)(2), the Commission on Local Governance for the
21st Century qualifies for an exemption from the need to develop a conflict of interest code. The
commission members are not required to complete the statement of economic interests. I trust
this answers your question sufficiently. If you have any questions, please feel free to contact me
at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

SGC/BT/jet

²Should this occur, the commission should seek further advice to determine if it still meets the criteria
permitting the exemption.
